seo_title: Eroding Trust in Real Speech: A Large-Scale Study of Human Audio…
meta_description: A recent study reveals that humans are increasingly distrusting authentic speech, shifting skepticism from fake samples to real ones.
body_html:
Human accuracy on fake samples barely changed, but accuracy on real samples dropped from 72.7% to 64.1%, according to the largest listening study on audio deepfake perception to date.

Participants increasingly distrust authentic speech, shifting skepticism from fake samples to real ones. Commercial and autoregressive language model systems proved hardest to detect (61.3 - 65.9%), while traditional seq2seq and flow-matching models remain easier to spot (75.4 - 76.8%). An ML detector maintained over 94.5% accuracy across all conditions.

System TypeAverage Accuracy on Fake SamplesAverage Accuracy on Real Samples

Commercial and Autoregressive Language Model Systems61.3 - 65.9%64.1% Traditional Seq2seq and Flow-Matching Models75.4 - 76.8%72.7%

Conclusion The primary threat posed by modern deepfakes may not be mere deception, but the erosion of trust in genuine audio.

source.title: Eroding Trust in Real Speech: A Large-Scale Study of Human Audio Deepfake Perception
source.summary: Researchers conducted a large-scale study on human perception of audio deepfakes, collecting data from 1,768 participants and 138 text-to-speech systems. The study found that while people's accuracy at detecting synthesized speech samples remained relatively stable, their ability to detect authentic speech decreased significantly. This suggests that the primary threat posed by modern deepfakes may not be convincing deception, but rather eroding trust in genuine audio.
